sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Snakes produce venom in modified salivary glands and inject it through fangs which pierce the skin. Many Australian snakes, including sea snakes, can cause life-threatening envenomation, so all suspected snake bites should be managed cautiously regardless of initial appearance.
- Life-threatening effects include:
  - Neurotoxic paralysis leading to respiratory failure
  - Coagulopathy with bleeding
  - Myotoxicity leading to renal injury
- Several snake species in WA pose a serious threat to humans, including tiger snakes, coastal taipans, mulga snakes, dugites, and death adders

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Puncture mark(s) or scratch
- Bite may be painless, without any visible marks (localized bruising or redness is uncommon in Australian snake bites)
- Headache, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain
- Swollen or tender lymphatic glands at groin/axilla of bitten limb
- Symptoms can resemble dysfunction of the CNS:
  - Confusion
  - Collapse
  - Visual disturbances
  - Drooping eyelids
  - Difficulty speaking
  - Swallowing or breathing
  - Weakness/paralysis
  - Respiratory weakness/arrest
  - Seizure

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- All suspected snake bite patients should be transported for medical assessment
- Most venom reaches the circulation via the lymphatic system, so early pressure immobilisation and strict rest are key first-aid measures for limb bites
- Paralysis may be prolonged, and respiratory or cardiac arrest management may need to continue until definitive care
- Definitive identification of envenomation and antivenom selection are hospital-based decisions; antivenom is only available at hospitals and should not be expected on site

**Do not:**
  - Walk the patient
  - Cut, wash, suck, or interfere with the bite site
  - Use an arterial tourniquet
  - Remove pressure bandages or splints before medical review unless specifically directed
  - Attempt to catch or kill the snake for identification

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Assist ventilations as required
- Manage cardiac arrest as per resuscitation guidelines
- Keep the patient completely still and reassure them
- For bites to a limb, apply a pressure immobilisation bandage and splint the limb as per clinical skill
- For bites to the trunk, apply firm local pressure where feasible and keep the patient still
- Monitor closely for respiratory depression, evolving weakness, and bleeding
- Do not remove bandages or splints before hospital assessment unless specifically directed
- Minimise movement, handling, and scene delays

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG and vascular access if clinically indicated and if this does not require excessive limb movement or compromise the pressure immobilisation strategy
- Transport all suspected snake bites to hospital and escalate early if envenomation signs are present
- Avoid unnecessary interventions that delay transport or disturb immobilisation

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Caring for the patients in the hours following envenomation (e.g. including inter-hospital transfer care) will include monitoring for signs of coagulopathy (bleeding from bite site or cannula) and renal impairment. This may include measuring urine output where possible
